summary: Joel Spolsky recounts the technological history of his blog, from Dave Winer's Manila platform in 1999 through his own CityDesk product to its final migration to WordPress. CityDesk was built on the bet that people would prefer desktop WYSIWYG blogging on Windows, which proved wrong as web-based platforms won out. The VB 6.0 codebase gradually stopped working on modern Windows, forcing Joel to maintain a Windows XP virtual machine just to publish. After years of persuasion from WordPress co-founder Matt Mullenweg, Joel finally migrated the site, crediting a team of developers who preserved 16 years of content and over 1000 links.
key_points: CityDesk was built on the assumption that desktop WYSIWYG editing would beat web-based blogging—a bet that proved monumentally wrong
Technology choices compound over time: VB 6.0 and Microsoft Jet created an increasingly unmaintainable system that eventually required a Windows XP VM to run
The early blogging ecosystem was DIY—cool kids wrote their own platforms, ran servers in closets, and 512MB of RAM was enough to survive Slashdot
Migrating 16 years of web content while preserving links, formatting, and structure is a monumental undertaking requiring significant human effort
money_quotes: In what turned out to be a monumentally wrong bet, I thought that people would want to blog on Windows, with all the slick WYSIWYG editing goodness that wasn't yet available in early versions of HTML.
The first time Slashdot linked to me it melted down. We upgraded the memory from 256M to 512M and it recovered.
In those days all the cool kids wrote their own blogging platforms.

So until recently, I had a virtual machine set up with Windows XP running in there, and a copy of CityDesk. That virtual machine runs on a modern Windows box which I don't use for any other purpose, so it lives under a desk in a cave somewhere and I have to remote desktop into it.
Matt Mullenweg over at WordPress has been trying to get me to move Joel on Software over to WordPress for so long it's not even funny.
key_insight: Technology bets that seem reasonable at the time—desktop over web, VB 6.0 over newer platforms—can become compounding liabilities that eventually force painful migrations.
